GUIDE FOR BASIC ACCOUNTING AND REPORTING 
FOR DIRECT LOAN PROGRAMS
WITHOUT COLLATERAL
IN FEDERAL CREDIT PROGRAM 
EFFECTIVE FISCAL YEAR 2017

GUIDE FOR BASIC ACCOUNTING AND REPORTING 
FOR DIRECT LOAN PROGRAMS
WITHOUT COLLATERAL
IN FEDERAL CREDIT PROGRAM
GENERAL FUND RECEIPT ACCOUNTS PROPRIETARY ACCOUNTS USED

1-1 To record the enactment of appropriations.10
Program Fund

1-2. The agency's requests for apportionment were approved by OMB without change,
and the apportionment was recorded.

1-3 Issued a blanket authorization to allot anticipated resources as they were realized, not 
to exceed the amount of the apportionment.

1-4. Borrowed from the Bureau of the Fiscal Service (Fiscal Service) the amount required 
to make $118,000 in loans. 11

1-5. Paid administrative expenses of $4,950. $4,000 was for employees and $950 to GSA. 
No undelivered orders were placed or accruals made before payment.12

1-6. Agreed to make $90,000 of loans, subject to the loan applicants meeting conditions 
placed on them.
To recognize the subsidy to be transferred from the program fund.
Collections from borrowers during the year were as follows:

1-8a. To record unearned fees collected in the financing fund. $80.00 in fees are 
unearned.
1-8b. To record the receipt of previously anticipated collections.
1-8c. To record anticipated resources as realized.

1-9. The collections received were paid to Fiscal Service. $700 was for interest on the
agency loan from Fiscal Service, and $10,280 was for principal.25
1-9a To record principal repayments to the Fiscal Service.
1-9b To record payment of interest not previously accrued.27
1-10b To unapportion anticipated subsidy (being used for repayment).
1-10c To record anticipated indefinite appropriation MAT.

1-10d To record anticipated repayments of debt using modification and MAT.
33
1-10e To record the return of excess borrowing to Fiscal Service (as a result of the modification).34

1-11 To record principal repayments to Fiscal Service and the via nonexpenditure 
transfers that was previously anticipated.38

1-12 Treasury credited $15 to the financing fund for interest the fund earned on its 
unused Fund Balance with Treasury during the year.

1-13 The interest collected from Fiscal Service was used to pay principal on theagency's 
loan from Fiscal Service.

1-15. Interest of $55 was accrued as receivable on the loans. Of that, $5 was related to 
loans, which were still performing.39
1-16. The allowance for subsidy was amortized.

1-17. As no further loans were disbursed during the year, the unneeded monies borrowed 
from Treasury were returned.42

1-18. The agency determined that $10 of loan principal and $20 of loan interest could not 
be collected, and wrote the receivables off. 43

1-20 Accrued interest of $30 was capitalized (converted to principal) on outstanding 
loans.

2-1b To record anticipated resources apportioned but not available for use until they are realized.
2-1c To record anticipated collections and anticipated non-expenditure payments toTreasury.
2-2 To record the enactment of the appropriations.

2-3 To record the realization of previously anticipated and apportioned programs subject
to apportionment.
2-5. The re-estimated subsidy was transferred from the program fund to the financing fund.
2-5a To record the re-estimated subsidy payable in the program account.
2-5b To record the disbursement of reestimate from the program fund to the financing fund.
2-5c To record collection of subsidy for an upward re-estimate in the financingaccount.

2-6 The financing fund used the subsidy collected to repay principal on its loan from 
Fiscal Service.

APPENDIX 1: ACCOUNTING FOR 
MANDATORY DIRECT LOAN PROGRAMS
BASIC DIFFERENCES BETWEEN DISCRETIONARY AND MANDATORY PROGRAMS
DIFFERENCES IN JOURNAL ENTRIES FOR MANDATORY PROGRAMS
1-2a. To record the enactment of appropriations.

1-2b. To record budgetary authority apportioned by the Office of Management and Budget 
and available for allotment.

1-10. After consultation and agreement with OMB, the agency decided to modify the 
terms of loans for some borrowers, reducing the present value of those loans by $1,000.

1-17. As no further loans were disbursed during the year, the unneeded monies borrowed 
from Treasury were returned.
